children.name: Rutilus
children.description: Roach — one of the most abundant freshwater fish in Europe, found in virtually every Swedish lake, river, and stream from coastal Skåne to the edge of the boreal zone. The roach (mört) is a schooling, omnivorous cyprinid that feeds on aquatic plants, invertebrates, and plankton. It is a critically important prey species for pike, zander, perch, cormorants, and ospreys — the 'rabbit of Swedish freshwater ecosystems'. In eutrophic, nutrient-rich lakes roach can reach enormous densities, and their grazing suppresses aquatic vegetation, contributing to algal bloom conditions. Roach form the base of virtually every Swedish freshwater food web.

children.name: Rutilus rutilus
children.description: Arguably the most numerous vertebrate in Sweden by total individuals. Distinguished by red eyes and orange-red lower fins. Hybridises readily with bream, rudd, and white bream — hybrids are common in eutrophic Swedish lakes. Primarily a prey species but popular with match anglers.

children.name: Rutilus meidingerii
children.description: A large, migratory roach endemic to the Danube drainage and adjacent Alpine lakes, reaching up to 50 cm. Makes seasonal migrations from deep lake habitats to river tributaries for spawning. Listed as Vulnerable due to dam construction blocking migration routes and hybridisation with introduced common roach.

children.name: Rutilus frisii
children.description: A large, anadromous roach of the Black Sea and Caspian basin rivers, reaching up to 75 cm and 3.5 kg. Undertakes extensive spawning migrations from coastal areas up major rivers such as the Danube, Dnieper, and Volga. Populations have declined sharply due to dam construction blocking migration routes.

children.name: Abramis
children.description: Bream — deep-bodied, laterally compressed cyprinids of slow rivers and lakes. The common bream (braxen) is one of Sweden's most familiar lake fish, forming large shoals that graze on benthic invertebrates by tilting almost vertically to vacuum up chironomid larvae from lake bottoms. Bream are important bioturbators: their grubbing in the sediment releases phosphorus, potentially accelerating eutrophication. Large bream ('slabs' in angler terminology) over 3–4 kg are a major target of specialist freshwater anglers. Bream are deep-bodied to avoid predation — it is difficult for a pike to swallow a fish almost as tall as it is wide.

children.name: Abramis brama
children.description: Extremely common in lowland Swedish lakes (braxen). Its benthic feeding releases sediment nutrients and can accelerate algal blooms. Large individuals can live 20+ years. Hybridises extensively with roach. Can reach 9 kg in productive waters.

children.name: Leuciscus
children.description: Ide, dace, and chub — a diverse group of European cyprinids found in rivers and lakes. The ide (id) is a handsome, fast-swimming cyprinid of clear, well-oxygenated Swedish rivers, often found in the company of grayling and trout. Its golden form ('orfe') is popular in ornamental ponds. The ide is migratory in many river systems, making large upstream spawning runs in spring — a spectacle that can be watched from bridges over Swedish spring rivers. Dace inhabit faster, cooler water than roach and are sensitive to siltation and eutrophication, making them useful water quality indicators.

children.name: Leuciscus idus
children.description: Swedish 'id' — a silvery, streamlined cyprinid of rivers and lakes. Spring spawning runs attract herons and ospreys. The golden form ('guld-id' or orfe) is popular in ornamental ponds. Tolerates slightly brackish water in the Baltic coastal zone.

children.name: Leuciscus leuciscus
children.description: A slender, active fish of fast-flowing rivers. More sensitive to pollution than roach or bream. An indicator of well-oxygenated rivers. Feeds at the surface, rising to flies in a manner that makes it beloved by fly fishers.

children.name: Leuciscus aspius
children.description: A large, predatory cyprinid unique among European members of the family in being an active, open-water piscivore that stuns prey with explosive tail-slaps at the surface. Can reach 12 kg in large rivers and is a major target of lure anglers. Found in rivers and large lakes across central and eastern Europe.

children.name: Scardinius
children.description: Rudd — vividly coloured cyprinids with intensely red fins and a golden sheen, among the most beautiful of European freshwater fish. Rudd are surface feeders, taking aquatic and terrestrial insects from the surface film with an upturned mouth — the opposite orientation to the downward-tilted bream. They are strongly associated with clear, weedy lakes and are considered indicators of good water quality. Swedish rudd (sarv) are found in lowland lakes with abundant submerged vegetation; their decline in some areas reflects the loss of aquatic plant communities to eutrophication.

children.name: Scardinius erythrophthalmus
children.description: Swedish 'sarv' — one of the most attractive European freshwater fish. Deep-bodied with brilliant red fins. Surface-feeding; readily takes dry flies. Associated with clear, well-vegetated lakes. Hybridises with roach; pure rudd are becoming rarer in eutrophic lakes.

children.name: Scardinius graecus
children.description: An endemic rudd found only in Lake Yliki and connected waters in Boeotia, central Greece. Distinguished from common rudd by its larger size and slightly different meristic counts. Threatened by water extraction and habitat degradation in its restricted range. Listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List.

children.name: Cyprinus
children.description: Common carp — one of humanity's oldest and most successfully cultured food fish, domesticated in China over 2,000 years ago and brought to Europe by the Romans. Wild carp are streamlined, golden-bronze fish of large, slow rivers and lakes; selective breeding has produced the familiar rounded, partially scaled forms of pond culture. In Sweden, carp are uncommon and largely restricted to warm, shallow lakes in Skåne and Öland. Wild carp are destructive bioturbators — their bottom-grubbing for invertebrates uproots aquatic vegetation and clouds the water, transforming clear lakes into turbid, weed-free basins. They are considered invasive or undesirable in many natural Swedish lake systems.

children.name: Cyprinus carpio
children.description: Domesticated over 2,000 years ago; one of the world's most widely cultured food fish. Wild populations in Sweden limited to warm Skåne and Öland lakes. Highly invasive in Australian freshwaters where introduced populations devastate aquatic vegetation and water clarity. The largest freshwater fish commonly encountered by Swedish anglers — can exceed 20 kg.

children.name: Cyprinus rubrofuscus
children.description: A large carp native to the Amur River basin and adjacent drainages of eastern Asia, sometimes considered a subspecies of common carp. Distinguished by its more elongate body and reddish fins. An important aquaculture species in China and Russia. Ancestor of several domesticated carp strains bred for cold-water tolerance.

children.name: Carassius
children.description: Crucian carps and goldfish — stocky, deep-bodied cyprinids highly tolerant of low oxygen and ice-covered lakes. Carassius species can survive winter beneath ice by switching to anaerobic metabolism and excreting ethanol rather than lactic acid. The crucian carp is native to Europe and Asia; the goldfish and gibel carp originate from East Asia but are now globally distributed through the aquarium trade and aquaculture.

children.name: Carassius carassius
children.description: Exceptionally tolerant of hypoxia — can survive frozen, oxygen-depleted ponds all winter by producing ethanol as a metabolic byproduct. In the absence of predators, crucian carp become deep-bodied and slab-shaped to deter pike; in predator-free ponds they remain slender. Native to Scandinavia and widespread across northern Europe.

children.name: Carassius auratus
children.description: The world's most popular ornamental fish, selectively bred in China for over 1,000 years into hundreds of varieties. Wild-type goldfish are olive-green, turning golden-orange only in clear, well-lit water. Feral populations established in ponds and waterways worldwide can become significant invasive pests, hybridising with native Carassius species.

children.name: Carassius gibelio
children.description: An invasive and highly successful cyprinid originating from Siberia and China, now widespread across Europe. Females can reproduce gynogenetically, using sperm from other cyprinid species to trigger egg development without fertilisation. This unusual reproductive strategy enables rapid population growth and makes it highly competitive with native species.

children.name: Tinca
children.description: Tench — a solitary, slow-moving cyprinid of weedy, warm lowland lakes and rivers. Tench have a distinctive olive-green colouration with tiny, deeply embedded scales coated in thick mucus, giving them a smooth, almost scaleless appearance. They are famously hardy, tolerating very low oxygen levels, and in medieval times were called the 'doctor fish' in the belief that their mucus could heal the wounds of other fish.

children.name: Tinca tinca
children.description: A stout, olive-green cyprinid of warm, weedy lakes and slow rivers; Swedish 'sutare'. Nocturnal and secretive, feeding on invertebrates in muddy substrates. Its thick mucus coating gives it a slimy feel; was historically called the 'doctor fish'. Tolerates very low oxygen, surviving conditions that kill most other cyprinids.

children.name: Barbus
children.description: Barbel — large, powerful rheophilic cyprinids adapted to fast, well-oxygenated rivers. Barbel have a characteristic ventral mouth with four fleshy barbels used to probe gravel riverbeds for invertebrates. They are among the strongest freshwater fish pound for pound, making them highly prized by anglers. Barbel require clean, gravelly rivers and are sensitive to pollution and habitat degradation.

children.name: Barbus barbus
children.description: A large, powerful cyprinid of clean, fast-flowing European rivers with gravel substrates. Uses four barbels around its downward-pointing mouth to locate invertebrates and crustaceans on the riverbed. One of the hardest-fighting freshwater fish for its size; hugely popular with river anglers. Sensitive to pollution and declining in degraded rivers.

children.name: Barbus capito
children.description: A large barbel of Central Asian rivers including the Amu Darya, Syr Darya, and their tributaries, reaching up to 1 m in length. An important commercial fish in Uzbekistan and Kazakhstan that is now seriously threatened by water abstraction for irrigation that has dramatically reduced flows in the Aral Sea basin.

children.name: Barbus callensis
children.description: A medium-sized barbel endemic to the coastal rivers of northern Algeria and Tunisia, one of the few native cyprinids of North Africa. Inhabits clear, fast-flowing streams in the Atlas Mountain foothills. Threatened by drought, water abstraction, and introduced species; listed as Vulnerable by IUCN.

children.name: Gobio
children.description: Gudgeon — small, bottom-dwelling cyprinids of clear, gravelly rivers and streams. Despite their modest size, gudgeon are ecologically important as prey fish and are sensitive indicators of river health. They have a pair of short barbels at the corners of the mouth and a mottled brown colouration that camouflages them against gravel riverbeds.

children.name: Gobio gobio
children.description: A small, gregarious bottom-dweller of clean gravel-bottomed rivers and streams. Forms tight shoals that dash for cover when alarmed. An important prey species for kingfishers, herons, and piscivorous fish. Its presence indicates good river quality. Feeds on invertebrates, detritus, and small crustaceans.

children.name: Gobio uranoscopus
children.description: A specialist gudgeon endemic to fast-flowing, well-oxygenated tributaries of the Danube, with eyes positioned more dorsally than the common gudgeon, allowing it to watch for predators while pressed against the substrate. Listed as Vulnerable due to its restricted range and sensitivity to habitat degradation from gravel extraction and channelisation.

children.name: Phoxinus
children.description: Minnows — tiny but brilliantly coloured cyprinids of cold, clear streams and mountain rivers. Male minnows in breeding condition develop vivid red bellies and green-gold flanks, making them among the most colourful native European freshwater fish. Minnows are highly social, forming dense shoals and communicating alarm through chemical signals released from damaged skin.

children.name: Phoxinus phoxinus
children.description: One of the most abundant small fish in cold, clear European streams. Shoals can number thousands of individuals that move in tight synchrony. Breeding males have brilliant red bellies and green iridescent flanks. Produce alarm pheromones from skin cells when injured, triggering flight responses in shoal-mates.

children.name: Phoxinus csikii
children.description: A small minnow recently distinguished from the common minnow complex, endemic to rivers of the eastern Adriatic drainage in Croatia and Bosnia. Genetic studies have revealed it to be a distinct species rather than a local variant. Its restricted distribution makes it potentially vulnerable to habitat change and pollution.

children.name: Squalius
children.description: Chub — robust, large-mouthed cyprinids of rivers and streams. Chub are generalist predators that take a wide variety of prey including insects, fish, frogs, and even small mammals that fall into the water. They are curious, opportunistic fish that will investigate almost any disturbance, making them both fascinating to observe and challenging to catch by stealth.

children.name: Squalius cephalus
children.description: A large, bold cyprinid of European rivers; Swedish 'färna'. Distinguished by its large mouth and silver-bronze scales with dark edges, creating a net-like pattern. An opportunistic generalist: takes insects, crayfish, berries, and small fish. Notoriously wary — a challenge to approach without disturbing. Reaches 4 kg in large rivers.

children.name: Squalius pyrenaicus
children.description: A medium-sized chub endemic to the Iberian Peninsula, particularly the Tagus and Guadiana basins. Adapted to the highly seasonal, Mediterranean-climate rivers of Portugal and Spain that shrink to isolated pools in summer. Faces threats from water abstraction, introduced largemouth bass predation, and drought intensified by climate change.

children.name: Alburnus
children.description: Bleak — slender, surface-dwelling cyprinids forming large, glittering shoals in lakes and slow rivers. Bleak have intensely silvery scales, historically harvested to make essence d'orient — the lustrous coating used in artificial pearl manufacture. They are important forage fish, sustaining populations of pike, perch, zander, and terns.

children.name: Alburnus alburnus
children.description: A small, intensely silver surface-dwelling cyprinid forming vast shoals in European lakes and rivers. Its scales were once harvested in huge quantities for artificial pearl manufacture (essence d'orient). An important forage fish for perch, pike, and terns. Can reach enormous population densities in eutrophic Swedish lakes.

children.name: Alburnus chalcoides
children.description: A larger, semi-anadromous bleak that enters rivers from the Caspian and Black Sea basins to spawn. Formerly abundant in the lower Danube and Volga deltas, this species has declined significantly following dam construction that impedes its spawning migrations. An important forage fish for sturgeons and Caspian terns.

children.name: Blicca
children.description: Silver bream (white bream) — a deep-bodied cyprinid superficially similar to common bream but smaller and with a distinctive grey-silver colour and orange-based paired fins. Often mistaken for juvenile common bream, but distinguishable by its larger eye relative to head size and paired fin coloration. A common component of mixed bream-roach shoals in eutrophic European lakes.

children.name: Blicca bjoerkna
children.description: Often called silver bream to distinguish it from common bream; Swedish 'björkna'. Smaller than common bream, rarely exceeding 35 cm. Deep-bodied and laterally compressed with orange-based pectoral and ventral fins. Found in lowland lakes and slow rivers throughout Europe. Hybridises frequently with roach and common bream.

children.name: Vimba
children.description: Vimba — an anadromous or potamodromous cyprinid that makes long spawning migrations up rivers from coastal and brackish water habitats. Vimba have an unusually protruding, piglike snout that overhangs the inferior mouth, adapted for grubbing invertebrates from gravel substrates. A prized sporting and food fish in eastern Europe and around the Baltic.

children.name: Vimba vimba
children.description: A distinctive cyprinid with a pronounced overhanging snout; Swedish 'vimma'. Makes major spawning migrations up Baltic rivers in spring and early summer. Breeding males develop vivid orange-red colouration on the belly and tubercles on the head. An important commercial and recreational fish in the eastern Baltic region.

children.name: Aspius
children.description: Asp — a large, predatory cyprinid unique among European members of the family in being an active, surface-oriented piscivore. The asp hunts by stunning small fish with violent tail-slaps at the surface before turning to engulf them — a hunting strategy that creates dramatic surface explosions audible from a distance. It occupies an ecological niche similar to a pike but in open water.

children.name: Aspius aspius
children.description: The only truly piscivorous cyprinid native to Europe. Hunts bleak and other small fish at the surface, stunning them with explosive tail-slaps before consuming them. Swedish 'asp' — found in large rivers and lakes in southern and central Sweden. Can reach 12 kg; a major target of lure anglers. Listed as Least Concern by IUCN.

children.name: Danio
children.description: Danios — small, fast-swimming cyprinids of South and Southeast Asian streams and rice paddies. Danios are among the most popular aquarium fish worldwide, and the zebrafish (Danio rerio) has become one of the most important model organisms in biomedical research. Their external fertilisation, transparent embryos, and rapid development make them invaluable for studying vertebrate genetics and development.

children.name: Danio rerio
children.description: One of the most important vertebrate model organisms in biological and medical research. Its transparent embryos, rapid generation time, and fully sequenced genome have made it indispensable for studying genetics, development, and disease. Named for its distinctive horizontal blue and silver stripes. Native to the Ganges and Brahmaputra river basins of South Asia.

children.name: Danio kyathit
children.description: A small, vividly coloured danio endemic to the Ayeyarwady (Irrawaddy) River drainage in northern Myanmar. Characterised by orange pectoral fins and bold horizontal stripes. Closely related to zebrafish and used in comparative developmental biology studies. Popular in the aquarium trade but rarely seen outside specialist collections.

children.name: Danio albolineatus
children.description: A popular aquarium fish from the rivers of Myanmar, Thailand, and the Malay Peninsula, named for its pearlescent iridescent sheen. Inhabits fast-flowing hill streams and river edges with clear water. Hardy and easily bred in captivity; one of the first tropical fish widely kept in the European aquarium hobby.

children.name: Labeo
children.description: Labeos — large South and Southeast Asian cyprinids characterised by fleshy, lobed lips adapted for scraping algae and organic matter from submerged rocks and substrates. Several species are among the most commercially important freshwater food fish in South Asia, with rohu being a cornerstone of freshwater aquaculture across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Labeo rohita
children.description: One of the most economically important freshwater fish in South Asia, forming a cornerstone of aquaculture in India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and Myanmar. A column feeder consuming phytoplankton, zooplankton, and submerged vegetation. Can reach 45 kg and is highly prized as a food fish. Major production exceeds one million tonnes per year from aquaculture.

children.name: Labeo catla
children.description: One of the fastest-growing freshwater fish in South Asia and a cornerstone of polyculture pond systems across India and Bangladesh. Distinguished by its large, upturned mouth and broad head, adapted for surface and column feeding on zooplankton and phytoplankton. Reaches up to 45 kg; the fourth most produced freshwater fish globally by aquaculture tonnage.

children.name: Labeo calbasu
children.description: A bottom-feeding labeo of large rivers and floodplains across the Indian subcontinent, characterised by orange-tipped fins. Feeds primarily on mud and detritus, playing an important role in benthic nutrient cycling. Widely cultured alongside rohu and catla in traditional South Asian polyculture systems.

children.name: Ctenopharyngodon
children.description: Grass carp — large, herbivorous cyprinids native to eastern Asia that have been introduced worldwide as a biocontrol agent against aquatic vegetation. Grass carp consume enormous quantities of aquatic plants — up to three times their body weight daily — making them useful for weed control in ponds and irrigation canals, but potentially devastating to natural aquatic ecosystems if they escape into wild waterways.

children.name: Ctenopharyngodon idella
children.description: The world's most-produced freshwater fish by aquaculture weight, with global production exceeding five million tonnes annually. A voracious herbivore that can consume its own body weight in aquatic vegetation daily. Introduced widely for weed control and food production; considered invasive in many regions where it escapes into natural waterways and strips aquatic vegetation.

children.name: Hypophthalmichthys
children.description: Silver and bighead carps — filter-feeding cyprinids that consume phytoplankton and zooplankton by straining water through specialised gill rakers. Native to large rivers of eastern Asia, they are now globally distributed through aquaculture and are considered highly invasive in North American waterways, where they outcompete native filter feeders and pose a physical hazard by leaping from the water when disturbed.

children.name: Hypophthalmichthys molitrix
children.description: A filter-feeding cyprinid that strains phytoplankton and fine particulate matter through densely packed gill rakers. One of the most produced food fish in aquaculture worldwide. Notorious in North American rivers for leaping spectacularly from the water when startled by boat engines — a hazard to boaters. Considered among the world's worst invasive fish species.

children.name: Hypophthalmichthys nobilis
children.description: A large filter-feeder reaching up to 1.4 m and 40 kg, native to the major rivers of eastern China. Distinguished from silver carp by its larger head relative to body size and preference for zooplankton over phytoplankton. Among the highest-volume aquaculture species globally; invasive in the Mississippi River basin where it competes with native paddlefish and other filter feeders.

children.name: Tor
children.description: Mahseer — large, powerful cyprinids of fast, rocky rivers in South and Southeast Asia. Mahseer are among the most revered freshwater sport fish in the world, capable of powerful runs in turbulent mountain rivers. Several species are threatened by overfishing, dam construction, and habitat loss. The golden mahseer is considered sacred in some parts of India and is actively protected in temple pools.

children.name: Tor tor
children.description: A large, powerful cyprinid of fast, rocky rivers in the Himalayan foothills and Indian peninsula. Can reach 54 kg and is considered one of the ultimate freshwater sport fish, capable of explosive runs in heavy current. Listed as Vulnerable by IUCN due to overfishing, dam construction, and habitat degradation. Revered in Hindu culture and protected in some temple pools.

children.name: Tor putitora
children.description: Perhaps the most celebrated freshwater sport fish in South Asia, prized for its fighting ability in the turbulent Himalayan rivers of Nepal, India, and Bhutan. Characterised by golden-bronze scales. Considered sacred in parts of the Himalayan foothills; communities around temples have protected river reaches where fish live undisturbed for generations. Listed as Endangered by IUCN.

children.name: Tor khudree
children.description: A large mahseer of peninsular Indian rivers, particularly the Western Ghats drainages including the Krishna and Cauvery systems. Reaches over 20 kg. Has suffered from intensive gill-netting, dynamite fishing, and dam construction. Active conservation programmes in the Cauvery River have shown some population recovery.

children.name: Pimephales
children.description: Fathead minnows and bullhead minnows — small North American cyprinids that play an outsized ecological role as forage fish and as the source of the first chemically characterised alarm substance (Schreckstoff). Fathead minnows are extensively used in toxicology testing and ecotoxicological bioassays, making them one of the most experimentally studied vertebrates in environmental science.

children.name: Pimephales promelas
children.description: A small, hardy North American cyprinid foundational to aquatic toxicology — the standard test organism for freshwater ecotoxicological bioassays under US EPA guidelines. Males develop a fleshy pad on the forehead used to clean nest sites under rocks, then guard eggs aggressively. Widely used as baitfish; can establish feral populations when released.

children.name: Pimephales notatus
children.description: A common North American cyprinid of clear streams and rivers from the Great Lakes basin to the Gulf Coast. Named for its distinctively blunt, rounded snout. An important forage fish for bass, walleye, and other game fish. Males guard nest sites under flat rocks and leaves; females deposit eggs on the underside of the substrate.

children.name: Pimephales vigilax
children.description: A small minnow of lowland rivers and streams of the central and southern United States, from the Missouri basin to the Gulf Coast. Tolerant of turbid, warm water with high siltation. Males develop a conspicuous black band on the dorsal fin and guard nests in cavities beneath debris or flat rocks. An important prey item for sunfish and bass in prairie stream communities.

children.name: Garra
children.description: Garra — a diverse genus of small cyprinids found across Africa, the Middle East, and South and Southeast Asia. Characterised by a sucking disc on the underside of the snout used to cling to rocks in fast-flowing water while scraping algae and biofilm. Several species are used in the spa industry for their behaviour of nibbling dead skin from human feet, earning the name 'doctor fish'.

children.name: Garra rufa
children.description: A small cyprinid native to river systems of the Middle East, particularly Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Iran. Famous for its use in fish spas, where it nibbles dead and softened skin from bathers' feet — a practice that spread globally from Turkish hot spring resorts. In its native rivers it feeds on algae and organic matter scraped from rocks. Wild populations are Vulnerable in their native range due to overcollection.

children.name: Garra gotyla
children.description: A widespread Garra of the Indus, Ganges, and Brahmaputra basins, found in fast-flowing hill streams from Pakistan to Myanmar. Its large, well-developed adhesive disc allows it to cling to boulders in powerful torrents while grazing algae. An ecologically important biofilm grazer in the oxygen-rich mountain rivers of the Himalayan foothills.

children.name: Garra waterloti
children.description: One of the most widely distributed African Garra species, found across the Sahel and savanna zone rivers of West Africa from Senegal to Niger. Uses its adhesive disc to graze algae and biofilm from submerged rocks in the fast-flowing reaches of large rivers. An important component of West African freshwater fish communities where it contributes to benthic productivity.

children.name: Chondrostoma
children.description: Nase — cyprinids with a distinctive cartilaginous, cutting lower lip adapted for scraping algae, diatoms, and organic matter from rock surfaces in fast, clean rivers. Nase are among the most sensitive European cyprinids to pollution and river regulation, making them reliable biological indicators of river quality. They undertake pronounced upstream spawning migrations in spring.

children.name: Chondrostoma nasus
children.description: A streamlined cyprinid of fast, well-oxygenated rivers across central Europe, named for its characteristic downturned, cartilage-edged mouth used to rasp algae from rocks. Makes significant upstream spawning migrations blocked by even minor weirs. A key indicator of good river quality; severely impacted by channelisation, gravel extraction, and low-head dams.

children.name: Chondrostoma toxostoma
children.description: A nase endemic to rivers of southern France draining to the Mediterranean, particularly the Rhône, Garonne, and Aude systems. Highly sensitive to summer low flows typical of Mediterranean-climate rivers, making it acutely vulnerable to water abstraction and drought. Listed as Vulnerable; captive breeding programmes have been established to support wild populations.

children.name: Pseudorasbora
children.description: Topmouth gudgeon — small, invasive cyprinids native to eastern Asia that have become one of the most damaging aquatic invasive species in Europe. Accidentally introduced through contaminated fish stocking, they spread rapidly due to early maturation, high reproductive rate, and tolerance of degraded habitats. They are vectors for a deadly parasitic fungus that threatens native cyprinids.

children.name: Pseudorasbora parva
children.description: A small, highly invasive cyprinid native to eastern China that has spread across most of Europe since the 1960s when it was accidentally introduced with grass carp consignments. A carrier of Sphaerothecum destruens, a lethal intracellular pathogen that causes mass mortality in native European cyprinids such as sunbleak and swamp minnow. Listed among the worst invasive species in Europe.

children.name: Schizothorax
children.description: Snowtrout — a large genus of high-altitude cyprinids adapted to the cold, fast rivers of the Tibetan Plateau, Himalayas, and Central Asian mountains. Often the dominant fish of mountain rivers above 2,000 m elevation where few other species survive. Distinguished by a row of enlarged scales along the lateral line and a lobed lower lip for scraping algae from rocks.

children.name: Schizothorax richardsonii
children.description: The most widely distributed snowtrout, ranging from the Indus to the Brahmaputra across the Himalayan foothills and Tibetan Plateau rivers up to 4,500 m elevation. An important food and commercial fish in mountain communities of Nepal, India, and China. Vulnerable to overfishing and increasingly to glacial melt altering river hydrology.

children.name: Schizothorax intermedius
children.description: A snowtrout of the Amu Darya and Syr Darya basins in Central Asia, once abundant in the rivers feeding the Aral Sea. Has suffered catastrophically from the desiccation of the Aral Sea and dramatic reduction of river flows through Soviet-era irrigation. Now restricted to the headwater mountain reaches of its former range.

children.name: Schizothorax plagiostomus
children.description: A snowtrout endemic to the Indus and upper Ganges drainages of the western Himalayas, found from Pakistan through northern India to Nepal at elevations of 1,000–3,500 m. Named for its somewhat obliquely positioned mouth. An important subsistence food fish in mountain communities of Kashmir and Himachal Pradesh.

children.name: Crossocheilus
children.description: Siamese algae eaters and related suckers — cyprinids of Southeast Asian rivers valued both as algae grazers in aquaria and as important components of tropical riverine ecosystems. They feed on algae, filamentous algae, and organic matter scraped from substrate surfaces. The Siamese algae eater is one of the few fish that will consume tough black beard algae, making it highly prized by aquarists.

children.name: Crossocheilus oblongus
children.description: A slender cyprinid native to the Chao Phraya and Mekong basins of Thailand and the Malay Peninsula, prized by aquarists for its unique ability to consume tough black brush algae that most other algae-eating fish avoid. Often confused with the false Siamese algae eater (Garra cambodgiensis). An active, fast-swimming fish that grazes continuously on biofilm and algae.

children.name: Crossocheilus reticulatus
children.description: A species from the Mekong and associated drainages of Southeast Asia, characterised by a reticulated pattern of dark-edged scales. Less commonly kept in aquaria than its relative C. oblongus but increasingly popular as a larger, more robust algae grazer. Inhabits flowing sections of rivers and streams with rocky or gravel substrates.

children.name: Dawkinsia
children.description: Filament barbs — a genus of ornate, colorful cyprinids endemic to peninsular India and Sri Lanka, many of which were formerly placed in Puntius. Several species develop elongated dorsal fin rays forming a distinctive filament in males. They are popular aquarium fish and important elements of Western Ghats riverine biodiversity, a globally threatened hotspot.

children.name: Dawkinsia filamentosa
children.description: A medium-sized barb endemic to the Western Ghats of southern India and Sri Lanka, named for the elongated filament extending from the dorsal fin of breeding males. Inhabits clear, fast-flowing streams and rivers in hilly terrain. A popular aquarium species. Listed as Least Concern but dependent on the continued integrity of Western Ghats forest watersheds.

children.name: Dawkinsia rohani
children.description: A brilliantly coloured barb endemic to a handful of rivers in the Nilgiri Hills of Tamil Nadu and Kerala in the Western Ghats biodiversity hotspot. Characterised by a vivid red body with black markings. Restricted to a small area of high-quality forest stream habitat and considered Endangered due to ongoing deforestation and water abstraction.

children.name: Puntius
children.description: Tiger barbs and relatives — a genus of small to medium Asian cyprinids following significant taxonomic revision. Many former 'Puntius' species have been moved to genera including Dawkinsia, Systomus, and Barbodes. The remaining Puntius are mostly small South Asian barbs. Tiger barbs are among the most popular aquarium fish globally, known for semi-aggressive fin-nipping behaviour in groups.

children.name: Puntius tetrazona
children.description: One of the most popular freshwater aquarium fish worldwide, native to rivers of Sumatra and Borneo. Characterised by four prominent black vertical bars on a golden-orange body, resembling a tiger's markings. Best kept in groups of six or more, where fin-nipping behaviour is directed within the group rather than at tank-mates. Widely farmed for the aquarium trade.

children.name: Puntius sophore
children.description: A widespread small barb across South and Southeast Asia from Pakistan to Myanmar, commonly found in slow-moving rivers, canals, ponds, and flooded rice paddies. One of the most abundant cyprinids in the lowland Ganges and Brahmaputra plains. Tolerant of degraded water quality, allowing it to persist in heavily modified agricultural landscapes.

children.name: Systomus
children.description: A genus of medium to large barbs from South and Southeast Asia, split from the former broad Puntius assemblage following molecular phylogenetic studies. Several species are popular aquarium fish; others are important food fish in their native regions. Many inhabit the freshwater systems of the Western Ghats and Sri Lanka, a globally significant freshwater biodiversity hotspot.

children.name: Systomus sarana
children.description: A large, commercially important barb native to the rivers and floodplains of the Indian subcontinent from Pakistan to Myanmar. Reaches up to 40 cm and is an important component of South Asian artisanal fisheries. Found in a wide range of habitats including rivers, lakes, and seasonal wetlands. Feeds on aquatic plants, insects, and organic detritus.

children.name: Systomus haasii
children.description: A medium-sized barb from rivers of the Malay Peninsula and Sumatra, inhabiting clear, forested blackwater streams. Listed as Vulnerable due to extensive deforestation in its native range causing siltation and water quality degradation of its preferred blackwater stream habitat.

children.name: Neolissochilus
children.description: Sebarau and related mahseer-like barbs — medium to large cyprinids of South and Southeast Asian rivers, often placed near the mahseers (Tor). Several species are popular angling targets and important food fish in Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula. They inhabit fast-flowing, well-oxygenated rivers and require clean water and intact riparian forests.

children.name: Neolissochilus soroides
children.description: A large, prized game fish native to rivers of the Malay Peninsula and Borneo, reaching up to 70 cm and 8 kg. Known as 'sebarau' in Malay, it is renowned for its fighting ability when hooked and is a flagship species for Malaysian freshwater sport fishing. Requires clean, forested rivers and is used as an indicator of watershed integrity.

children.name: Mylopharyngodon
children.description: Black carp — a large, mollusc-eating cyprinid of eastern China monotypic in its genus. Black carp have powerful, molar-like pharyngeal teeth adapted for crushing snail and mussel shells, making them highly specialised predators of freshwater molluscs. They are important in controlling snail populations in Chinese fish ponds, as the snails are intermediate hosts of parasitic flukes.

children.name: Mylopharyngodon piceus
children.description: A large cyprinid native to major river systems of eastern China, reaching up to 1.8 m and 70 kg. Its molar-like pharyngeal teeth can crush thick-shelled freshwater molluscs. Cultured in China for centuries to control snail populations that harbour parasitic flukes. Introduced to North American aquaculture and considered seriously invasive in the Mississippi basin.

children.name: Megalobrama
children.description: Wuchang bream and Chinese breams — deep-bodied, herbivorous cyprinids of eastern China's major river systems. Megalobrama species are among the most important freshwater aquaculture fish in China, contributing millions of tonnes annually. They feed primarily on aquatic vegetation and organic detritus and are well adapted to the slow, productive lowland lakes and rivers of the Yangtze and Pearl River basins.

children.name: Megalobrama amblycephala
children.description: One of China's most economically important freshwater food fish, with annual aquaculture production exceeding 700,000 tonnes. Named after Wuchang (now Wuhan) on the Yangtze River, where it has been a celebrated food fish for centuries. A herbivore of lake margins and slow rivers, feeding on aquatic plants, algae, and detritus. Widely cultured in polyculture ponds alongside grass and silver carp.

children.name: Sinocyclocheilus
children.description: Golden-line fish and cavefish — a remarkable genus of Chinese cyprinids that includes numerous cave-adapted species found in the karst limestone systems of Guizhou, Yunnan, and Guangxi provinces. Cave-dwelling species show dramatic adaptations including eye reduction or complete blindness, loss of pigmentation, and elongated sensory organs. The genus has diversified explosively in the geologically complex karst landscapes of southwestern China.

children.name: Sinocyclocheilus rhinocerous
children.description: A striking cave-dwelling cyprinid from underground rivers in Guizhou Province, China, named for a prominent horn-like projection on the snout whose function remains debated. Completely depigmented and blind, with highly developed lateral-line sensory systems for navigating in total darkness. Critically endangered due to its restricted range and vulnerability to groundwater pollution from agriculture.

children.name: Sinocyclocheilus anatirostris
children.description: A cave-adapted cyprinid from Guangxi Province with an extraordinary flattened, duck-bill-shaped snout, one of the most bizarre morphologies in the family. Eyes are vestigial and functionless. The flattened rostrum is believed to be an adaptation for probing crevices in the cave substrate for invertebrate prey. Critically endangered and known from only a few cave systems.

children.name: Romanogobio
children.description: Kessler's gudgeon and related gudgeons — a diverse assemblage of small bottom-dwelling cyprinids distributed across the Danube basin and Black Sea drainages of central and eastern Europe. Recently segregated from Gobio based on molecular and morphological evidence. Many species are restricted to specific river systems and are sensitive to gravel extraction and habitat modification.

children.name: Romanogobio kesslerii
children.description: A small gudgeon endemic to fast-flowing, gravel-bottomed reaches of the middle Danube and its tributaries, named after the Russian zoologist Karl Kessler. Requires clean, well-oxygenated water with minimal siltation. Listed as Vulnerable in the IUCN Red List due to channelisation, gravel extraction, and increased turbidity from agricultural runoff.

children.name: Leptobarbus
children.description: Sultan fish and related large barbs — medium to large cyprinids of the major river systems of Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula. Leptobarbus species are characterised by a slender body, relatively large scales, and a predilection for fruit fallen from overhanging riparian trees. They are important seed dispersers and play a significant ecological role in forest rivers.

children.name: Leptobarbus hoevenii
children.description: A large barb from the Mekong, Chao Phraya, and Kapuas river systems of Southeast Asia, nicknamed 'mad barb' for its energetic, erratic behaviour when hooked or disturbed. Can reach 60 cm. Feeds heavily on fallen fruits, making it an important seed disperser in riparian forest systems. Threatened by overfishing and deforestation degrading its forest river habitat.

children.name: Cyclocheilichthys
children.description: Helicopter barbs and related species — medium-sized cyprinids of Southeast Asian river systems from Thailand and Cambodia to Borneo and Sumatra. Several species are characterised by long barbels and a preference for clear, flowing water. They are important components of Mekong River fisheries and are increasingly threatened by the construction of large hydroelectric dams.

children.name: Cyclocheilichthys apogon
children.description: A medium-sized barb widespread in rivers and floodplain lakes of the Mekong, Chao Phraya, and Malay Peninsula. Despite its common name referencing reduced barbels, it has short but functional barbels. An important component of subsistence and commercial fisheries in Cambodia, Thailand, and Vietnam. Able to tolerate the seasonal flood-drought regime of the Mekong floodplain.

children.name: Barbodes
children.description: Large-scaled barbs — a genus of medium to large barbs primarily from the Philippines, Borneo, and Sulawesi, with some species in mainland Southeast Asia. Barbodes species tend to be larger and more deep-bodied than Puntius barbs. Several Philippine endemic species are seriously threatened by deforestation, sedimentation, and introduced predators.

children.name: Barbodes gonionotus
children.description: A large, commercially important barb of Southeast Asian rivers and floodplains from Thailand to Java, widely cultured in ponds and rice paddies across the region. Reaches 45 cm and is an important food fish across Indochina. Tolerant of poor water quality, which, combined with its fast growth rate, has made it a common aquaculture species in small-scale rural farms.

children.name: Barbodes truncatulus
children.description: A small barb endemic to rivers of the Philippine island of Luzon. Like many Philippine endemic freshwater fishes, it faces severe threats from deforestation causing sedimentation of its clear-stream habitat, as well as from introduced tilapia and snakehead predators. Assessed as Vulnerable by IUCN.

children.name: Capoeta
children.description: Capoeta — scrapers of the Middle East and Central Asia, characterised by a hardened, keratinised lower lip edge used to rasp algae and diatoms from rocks in fast-flowing streams. The genus is centred on the rivers of Turkey, Iran, and the Levant, with species also in Central Asia. Several species have extremely restricted ranges and are threatened by water abstraction in the arid regions they inhabit.

children.name: Capoeta tinca
children.description: A medium-sized scraper widespread in rivers of Anatolia, the Levant, and the upper Tigris-Euphrates systems. Uses its hardened lower lip to scrape epilithic algae and biofilm from rock surfaces in fast, rocky streams. An important component of Middle Eastern freshwater fish communities, and a traditional food fish in Turkey and Lebanon.

children.name: Capoeta damascina
children.description: A scraper native to rivers of the Levant, Mesopotamia, and the Arabian Peninsula, including the Jordan, Orontes, Tigris, and Euphrates systems. One of the most broadly distributed Capoeta species. Named after Damascus. Faces increasing pressure from dam construction and water abstraction across the stressed river systems of the Middle East.

children.name: Capoeta capoeta
children.description: A widespread scraper of rivers draining to the Caspian Sea across Iran, Azerbaijan, Georgia, and Armenia, with several recognised subspecies occupying different drainage systems. Characterised by its sharp-edged lower jaw for rasping algae. An important commercial and subsistence fish across the Caucasus and northern Iran, where it is known as 'sefid mahi' in some regions.

children.name: Tanichthys
children.description: White Cloud Mountain minnows — tiny, brilliantly coloured cyprinids from cool mountain streams of southern China and Vietnam. One of the most popular cold-water aquarium fish, and remarkably, were believed extinct in the wild for decades before rediscovery. They were named after a Chinese boy scout, Tan Kam Fai (Tan in Cantonese), who first brought specimens to a biologist.

children.name: Tanichthys albonubes
children.description: A tiny, exquisitely coloured minnow native to cool mountain streams on White Cloud Mountain (Baiyun Shan) near Guangzhou, China. Declared extinct in the wild in the 1980s due to tourism impacts, but later rediscovered at several sites in Guangdong and Vietnam. Hardy enough to be kept in unheated aquaria in temperate climates. Named after the Chinese scout Tan Kam Fai who discovered it.
